planting.note: Set rhizomes into pond baskets once water has warmed in spring.

directions: Plant the rhizome horizontally in a wide aquatic basket of heavy loam, crown just exposed, and top with pea gravel to keep soil from clouding the water. Lower the basket so 8 to 18 inches of water covers the crown. Blush-pink, fragrant blooms open across summer once leaves reach the surface; remove yellowing pads to keep the plant tidy.

planting.note: Divide and pot dormant rhizomes in spring as the water warms.

directions: Press the rhizome into loam-filled basket soil at a shallow angle with the growing tip above the surface, then gravel-mulch and submerge. Allow 10 to 18 inches of water over the crown in a spot with six or more hours of sun. Deep rose flowers deepen in color with each successive day of bloom; deadhead spent flowers below the water to discourage rot.

planting.note: Pot in spring and lower gradually as new leaves reach for the surface.

directions: Firm the rhizome into a basket of dense garden soil with the crown proud of the surface, cap with gravel, and settle it under 8 to 18 inches of water. This free-flowering variety holds its clear pink cups open longer into the afternoon than most. Feed with an aquatic tablet monthly during the growing season for continuous bloom.

planting.note: Start the rhizome in a basket once nights stay above freezing.

directions: Lay the rhizome across loam in a wide pan, tip exposed, gravel over the soil, and lower under 12 to 18 inches of water in full sun. Vivid canary-yellow blooms stand slightly above the pads and appear reliably all summer. Divide every two or three years when leaves begin crowding the surface.

planting.note: Plant the running rhizome in spring in a roomy basket.

directions: This odorata-type has a running rhizome, so plant it lengthwise along the basket with the tip at one end and cover lightly with soil and gravel. Submerge under 8 to 16 inches of water in a sunny position. Star-shaped, mottled-leaf plants carry soft sulphur-yellow, fragrant flowers held just above the water.

planting.note: Pot up in spring and place in the warmest, sunniest part of the pond.

directions: Set the rhizome shallowly in a large basket of heavy loam, gravel the top, and submerge under 12 to 18 inches of water. Prized for its large, clear yellow flowers with long petals, it needs strong sun and warmth to flower freely. Feed monthly and give it space, as this cultivar spreads vigorously.

planting.note: A compact grower suited to smaller pools; pot in spring.

directions: Plant the small rhizome in a basket of loam with the crown just at the surface and cover with gravel. Its modest spread makes it a good choice for tubs or small ponds under 6 to 15 inches of water. Fragrant crimson-red flowers with orange-red stamens open freely through summer.

planting.note: Compact and free-flowering; ideal for small ponds and tubs.

directions: Firm the rhizome into a small aquatic basket, cap with gravel, and lower under 6 to 15 inches of water in full sun. The soft lilac-rose, fragrant flowers deepen in tone as they age over their several-day life. Keep it in a container to restrain spread and lift to divide every few seasons.

planting.note: A small, tub-friendly red that pots up easily in spring.

directions: Set the rhizome in a loam basket with the crown at the surface, gravel over it, and place under 6 to 15 inches of water. This dwarf-to-medium lily bears wine-red, cup-shaped flowers with contrasting stamens. Its restrained size suits patio containers and small water features.

planting.note: Pot the rhizome in spring for a medium to large pond.

directions: Plant horizontally in heavy loam with the growing tip exposed, gravel the surface, and lower under 10 to 18 inches of water. Bright cherry-red flowers flecked with white sit above the pads on this vigorous grower. Give it full sun and a monthly aquatic feed to keep blooms coming.

planting.note: One of the darkest hardy reds; pot up as water warms.

directions: Bed the rhizome in a basket of dense loam, crown at the surface, and finish with gravel before submerging under 10 to 18 inches of water. Its deep blood-red, almost blackish flowers rise over purple-tinged young leaves. Full sun brings out the richest flower color.

planting.note: Deep red flowers over bronze-purple young foliage; pot in spring.

directions: Plant the rhizome in loam at a shallow angle, tip proud of the soil, and gravel before lowering under 10 to 18 inches of water. Dark crimson flowers with yellow-orange stamens open over leaves that emerge purple and mature to green. Site in full sun for the deepest coloring and steadiest bloom.

planting.note: A richly colored changeable red; pot the rhizome in spring.

directions: Set the rhizome into heavy basket soil, crown at the surface, cover with gravel, and submerge under 10 to 18 inches of water. Flowers open deep rosy-red and darken with age, with mottled young leaves adding interest. Give it full sun and remove faded blooms to keep the display fresh.

planting.note: Long-petalled glowing red; pot up in spring for a medium pond.

directions: Plant the rhizome shallowly in a loam-filled basket, gravel the surface, and lower under 10 to 18 inches of water in full sun. The luminous red, star-form flowers hold their petals in a slightly incurved cup and stay open into late afternoon. Feed monthly and divide when the container fills.

planting.note: Large star-shaped rose flowers; pot the rhizome in spring.

directions: Bed the rhizome across a wide basket of loam, tip above the soil, and cover with gravel before submerging under 12 to 18 inches of water. Wide, star-form flowers open rose-pink and are streaked and flecked with deeper crimson. This free bloomer thrives in full sun and a generous container.

planting.note: Cupped rose-pink flowers that darken with age; pot in spring.

directions: Plant the rhizome in a large basket of heavy soil, crown exposed, gravel over it, and lower under 12 to 18 inches of water. The peony-shaped flowers open rosy-pink stippled with carmine and deepen each day they are open. A reliable, vigorous variety for larger ponds in full sun.

planting.note: A changeable red-and-orange lily for medium pools; pot in spring.

directions: Set the rhizome in a basket of loam with the crown at the surface, top with gravel, and place under 9 to 16 inches of water. Flowers blend brick-red and orange over heavily mottled young foliage, shifting tone as they age. Its medium size fits mid-sized ponds and larger tubs in full sun.

planting.note: A color-changing yellow-to-red lily; pot the rhizome in spring.

directions: Plant the rhizome in loam with the tip proud of the soil, gravel the surface, and lower under 9 to 16 inches of water in full sun. Flowers open soft apricot-yellow and deepen through orange to coppery-red over three days, so several shades show at once. This compact changeable is well suited to smaller ponds.

planting.note: Orange-red changeable with mottled foliage; pot in spring.

directions: Bed the rhizome in a loam basket, crown at the surface, cover with gravel, and submerge under 9 to 16 inches of water. Cup-shaped flowers glow orange-red with a bronze cast over purple-blotched young leaves. It performs best with full sun and a monthly feeding through summer.

planting.note: A small changeable orange lily ideal for tubs; pot in spring.

directions: Plant the small rhizome in a compact basket of loam, gravel the surface, and place under 6 to 15 inches of water. Its little flowers pass through apricot, orange and coppery-red, held over lightly mottled leaves. The modest spread makes it a favorite for patio containers and small ponds.

planting.note: A yellow-apricot changeable for small to medium pools; pot in spring.

directions: Set the rhizome into loam basket soil with the crown at the surface, cap with gravel, and lower under 8 to 16 inches of water. Flowers open pale sulphur-yellow and warm to apricot and rosy tones with age, over mottled young foliage. Provide full sun and steady feeding for the best succession of blooms.

planting.note: Large fragrant pink; pot the rhizome in spring for a big pond.

directions: Plant the rhizome in a large basket of heavy loam, gravel the surface, and submerge under 12 to 24 inches of water. Big, many-petalled, fragrant peach-pink flowers float among the pads and open reliably all season. This vigorous variety wants full sun and plenty of room to spread.

planting.note: Grown as much for variegated foliage as bloom; pot in spring.

directions: Bed the rhizome in loam with the crown at the surface, gravel over it, and lower under 10 to 18 inches of water in full sun. Its leaves are splashed with cream, pink, bronze and green, making it a standout even between the soft pink, fragrant flowers. Give it bright light to keep the leaf variegation vivid.

planting.note: A free-flowering red for medium pools; pot the rhizome in spring.

directions: Plant the rhizome shallowly in a basket of dense loam, gravel the top, and submerge under 10 to 18 inches of water. Fragrant, currant-red flowers open in profusion and tolerate a little more shade than most reds. It is one of the most dependable bloomers for a mid-sized pond.

planting.note: Fragrant double pink; pot the rhizome in spring.

directions: Set the rhizome into a large loam basket, crown at the surface, gravel over it, and lower under 12 to 18 inches of water. Double, star-shaped, richly fragrant pink flowers are held above the leaves through the season. Full sun and a monthly aquatic feed keep this popular variety blooming well.

planting.note: A vigorous yellow for deeper, larger ponds; pot in spring.

directions: Plant the rhizome in a large basket of heavy loam, gravel the surface, and submerge under 18 to 30 inches of water. This strong grower produces small semi-double yellow flowers that often stand well above the pads, and it tolerates deeper water than many. It sometimes forms viviparous plantlets on its leaves that can be potted up.

planting.note: A compact orange-red changeable for tubs; pot in spring.

directions: Plant the small rhizome in a compact loam basket, gravel the surface, and lower under 6 to 15 inches of water. Flowers open apricot and change through orange to coppery-red, held over purple-mottled young leaves. Its small footprint makes it a good choice for tubs and small ponds in full sun.

planting.note: A large blended peach-and-yellow changeable; pot in spring.

directions: Bed the rhizome in a wide basket of loam, crown at the surface, gravel over it, and submerge under 12 to 18 inches of water. Large flowers blend soft yellow, peach and pink with a rosy edge that deepens as they age. It needs full sun and a roomy container to reach its full size.

planting.note: A fragrant peach-toned hardy; pot the rhizome in spring.

directions: Plant the rhizome in a basket of heavy loam, crown just at the surface, gravel it over, and lower under 12 to 18 inches of water. Fragrant flowers open in warm peach-and-apricot shades that soften as they mature, over lightly speckled foliage. Give it full sun and monthly feeding for continuous summer bloom.

planting.note: A large cupped red for full-sized ponds; pot in spring.

directions: Set the rhizome into a large basket of dense loam, gravel the surface, and submerge under 12 to 18 inches of water. Big, cup-shaped crimson flowers with white-flecked outer petals sit above the pads on this strong grower. Full sun and ample room bring out its best, freely produced blooms.

planting.note: Wait until pond water holds above 70 F before planting tropical tubers.

directions: Pot the tuber in a basket of loam with the growing tip exposed, gravel the surface, and submerge under 8 to 16 inches of warm water. This day-blooming tropical carries semi-double, many-petalled violet-purple flowers held above the pads. Warmth is essential; lift and store the tuber over winter where frost occurs.

planting.note: Plant once water is reliably warm in late spring or early summer.

directions: Set the tuber in a loam-filled basket with the crown proud of the soil, gravel over it, and lower under 8 to 16 inches of warm water in full sun. Star-shaped, richly fragrant deep blue-purple flowers rise above the leaves during the day. Feed regularly through the warm months and overwinter the tuber indoors in cold climates.

planting.note: A vigorous purple day-bloomer; plant in warm water in early summer.

directions: Pot the tuber in heavy loam with the tip exposed, cap with gravel, and submerge under 8 to 18 inches of warm water. Fragrant, rich purple to magenta flowers are held above the leaves and open through the day all season. This strong grower blooms freely in full sun and should be overwintered frost-free.

planting.note: Grown for mottled foliage and magenta bloom; plant in warm water.

directions: Plant the tuber in a loam basket, growing tip above the soil, gravel the surface, and lower under 8 to 16 inches of warm water. Its leaves are boldly mottled in purple and green, setting off day-blooming rose-magenta, fragrant flowers. Provide full sun and regular feeding, and store the tuber frost-free over winter.

planting.note: A compact golden-yellow day tropical; plant in warm water.

directions: Pot the tuber in loam with the crown exposed, gravel over it, and submerge under 8 to 16 inches of warm water in full sun. Deep golden-yellow, day-blooming flowers stand well above the mottled pads and remain freely produced through summer. Its compact habit suits smaller ponds and tubs; overwinter the tuber indoors where frost occurs.

planting.note: A large blue day tropical; plant when water is warm.

directions: Set the tuber into a basket of loam with the tip exposed, gravel the top, and lower under 10 to 18 inches of warm water. Large, fragrant, sky-blue flowers with golden centers are held above the pads and open through the day. Give full sun and steady feeding, and keep the tuber frost-free through winter.

planting.note: A fragrant white day tropical; plant in warm summer water.

directions: Pot the tuber in loam with the growing tip above the soil, gravel the surface, and submerge under 8 to 18 inches of warm water. Large, pure white, star-shaped, fragrant flowers rise above the leaves and open through the day. This vigorous white wants full sun; overwinter its tuber frost-free in cold regions.

planting.note: A night-blooming red tropical; plant when water is warm.

directions: Plant the tuber in a large basket of heavy loam with the tip exposed, gravel over it, and submerge under 10 to 18 inches of warm water. This night bloomer opens deep pinkish-red, fragrant flowers in the evening that stay open through the following morning. Give full sun during the day, feed regularly, and overwinter the tuber frost-free.

planting.note: A large night-blooming red; plant in warm water in early summer.

directions: Set the tuber into a large basket of loam with the crown exposed, gravel the surface, and lower under 12 to 24 inches of warm water. Big, fragrant deep red flowers open in the evening over bronzy leaves and hold through the morning hours. This vigorous night bloomer needs full sun, ample space, and frost-free winter storage.

planting.note: A large night-blooming white; plant in warm water in summer.

directions: Pot the tuber in a large basket of heavy loam, growing tip exposed, gravel over it, and submerge under 12 to 24 inches of warm water. Very large, creamy-white flowers open in the evening over bronze-green leaves and remain open into the following morning. Provide full sun through the day, feed generously, and store the tuber frost-free over winter.

planting.note: Set tubers into pond containers once water has warmed after the last frost.

directions: Press the tuber horizontally into heavy loam in a wide, holeless container, leaving the growing tip exposed and topping with gravel to keep it from floating. Submerge so two to six inches of water covers the soil and give full sun for the small white cups this dwarf produces. Blooms typically open in early to midsummer once foliage stands well above the surface.

planting.note: Plant dormant tubers after frost when pond water reaches the sixties.

directions: Lay the brittle tuber just under the soil surface in a broad pot of clay loam, weighting the tip with pea gravel and covering with two to eight inches of water. This compact variety opens fragrant lavender-pink flowers that fade paler with age, so a warm sunny site speeds bud set. Expect the first blooms in early summer, followed by decorative seed pods.

planting.note: Start tubers after the last frost in a large, deep container.

directions: Give this vigorous grower a wide tub of rich loam and submerge the planted tuber under four to twelve inches of water. Full sun and warm summers bring large soft-pink blooms held high on strong stems, opening over several weeks in midsummer. Fertilize with aquatic tabs monthly during active growth for the fullest flowering.

planting.note: Pot tubers once frost has passed and water has warmed.

directions: Settle the tuber into heavy garden soil in a broad container, anchor the tip with gravel, and cover with two to ten inches of water. This pure-white variety flowers freely in full sun, with pointed petals that give the blooms an elegant look through midsummer. Keep the water level steady and feed lightly for repeat bloom.

planting.note: Plant tubers after frost in a large aquatic basket or tub.

directions: Bed the tuber shallowly in dense clay loam, cap with gravel, and submerge under four to twelve inches of water in full sun. This old red-flowered form opens deep rosy blooms that pale slightly as they age, giving weeks of color from early summer. Deep, warm containers and monthly feeding support its tall, full growth.

planting.note: Start tubers in a smaller tub after the last frost for this compact lotus.

directions: Lay the tuber just below the soil in a container of heavy loam and cover with two to eight inches of water. Valued for its cupped, tulip-shaped white flowers that never fully open, this smaller lotus suits tubs and small ponds in full sun. Blooms appear across midsummer and give way to ornamental seed heads.

planting.note: Pot the tubers after frost in a deep, wide basin.

directions: Press the tuber horizontally into clay loam, weight the tip with gravel, and submerge under four to twelve inches of water in full sun. This heirloom bears very full, many-petaled rose-pink flowers that make a striking summer display. Because the doubled blooms rarely set seed, propagate by dividing tubers in early spring.

planting.note: Divide and plant in spring or just after flowering.

directions: Set rhizomes in rich, acidic muck in shallow standing water of one to four inches or in constantly saturated soil. Unlike many irises this species thrives with its roots permanently flooded and opens broad blue-violet flowers in early summer. Divide congested clumps every few years after bloom to keep it vigorous.

planting.note: Plant divisions at the water's edge in spring or fall.

directions: Bed the rhizomes in boggy soil or shallow water at the pond margin where roots stay wet through the season. This native produces soft blue to violet flowers with a yellow signal in late spring and adapts to full sun or light shade. Lift and divide crowded rhizomes every three to four years to renew flowering.

planting.note: Lay rhizome sections in shallow water or bog mud in spring.

directions: Press the creeping rhizomes into mucky soil or shallow water up to a few inches deep at the pond edge, where they spread into a low mat. Three-part leaves resembling broad beans back short spikes of fringed, star-shaped white flowers in late spring. Give it full sun to light shade and acidic conditions, and thin the spreading stems as needed.

planting.note: Plant in a submerged pot after the water has warmed.

directions: Pot the plant in loam and submerge so about twelve to twenty-four inches of water covers the crown, letting round green leaves float on the surface. Frilly, feathery white flowers with a yellow center open in warm weather, each lasting a day. Treat it as a tropical and overwinter indoors or as a houseplant where frost occurs.

planting.note: Plant in deep, wet bog soil in spring.

directions: Plant in deep, rich, permanently wet muck at the edge of a stream or bog in full sun to part shade, allowing space for the huge leaves. In early spring it opens bright yellow spathes, faintly musky, before the paddle-shaped foliage expands to enormous size. It is slow to establish and resents disturbance, so choose a permanent boggy site.
